faq.q: What is entropy in simple terms?
faq.a: Entropy measures disorder or energy dispersal in a system. The second law of thermodynamics states the universe's entropy always increases (ΔS ≥ 0).

faq.q: When do I use the ΔS = Q/T formula?
faq.a: This formula applies to reversible processes at constant temperature, like phase changes (melting, boiling) and isothermal expansion.

faq.q: What's the entropy change when 1 kg of ice melts?
faq.a: At 0°C (273K), with a heat of fusion of 334 kJ/kg: ΔS = 334,000 J ÷ 273 K ≈ 1,224 J/K.

faq.q: Why does entropy increase during phase changes?
faq.a: Phase changes increase molecular disorder. Solids have ordered structure; liquids allow more movement; gases have maximum disorder—resulting in higher entropy.

faq.q: Can I use Celsius instead of Kelvin?
faq.a: No. The formula requires absolute temperature (Kelvin). Using Celsius would produce incorrect results because ΔS depends on the ratio, not just temperature differences.

faq.q: What's the difference between entropy and enthalpy?
faq.a: Enthalpy (ΔH) measures heat absorbed or released. Entropy (ΔS) measures disorder increase. For phase changes: ΔS = ΔH / T.

faq.q: What about boiling or evaporation entropy?
faq.a: Evaporation produces larger ΔS than melting. At 100°C (373K), water's heat of vaporization (~2,260 J/g) generates much higher entropy change per unit mass.

faq.q: Is this calculator accurate for homework or exams?
faq.a: Yes, it uses the standard formula validated internationally. Results are for reference. For critical decisions, consult your instructor or a professional.
